comments.body: Appstore concept can be a standalone Apache project on its own.  Before proposing this as independent project, I like to gather input from community to see if this feature is worthwhile to implement as a YARN sub-project or it would be better to developed as an independent project.

Thanks for working on this, [~eyang]. The catalog looks like a promising feature. I have started reviewing patch 18. Here is a first round of comments:
 - Service catalog might be a better name, since the catalog handles YARN services and not arbitrary applications. In that case I’d suggest moving the module to hadoop-yarn-applications/hadoop-yarn-services/hadoop-yarn-services-catalog.
 - NOTICE says “binary distribution of this product bundles binaries of ___” but it should be “source distribution bundles ___.” Also, this information should go in LICENSE instead of NOTICE for non-ASLv2 permissive licenses (see [http://www.apache.org/dev/licensing-howto.html#permissive-deps]). It would be helpful to include the path(s) to the dependencies, so it’s easier to tell when included source code has proper handling in LICENSE and NOTICE and when it doesn’t. I have not checked whether all the external code added in this patch has been included properly in L&N yet.
 - There are files named bootstrap-hwx.js and bootstrap-hwx.min.js.
 - 8080 is a difficult port. If you ran the catalog on an Ambari-installed cluster, it would fail if it came up on the Ambari server host. Does the app catalog need to have net=host? If not, the port wouldn’t be a problem.
 - I don’t think we should set net=host for the examples, because that setting is not recommended for most containers. Also, for the services using 8080, they could run into conflicts when net=host.
 - I think it would be better to have fewer examples in samples.xml and make sure that they all work (the pcjs image doesn’t exist on dockerhub, and we might not want to seem to be “endorsing” all the example images that are currently included). Maybe just include httpd and Jenkins? Registry would also be a reasonable candidate, but it didn’t work when I tried it with insecure limit users mode (it failed to make a dir when pushing an image to it; maybe would work as a privileged container with image name library/registry:latest).
 - entrypoint.sh needs modifications to make insecure mode possible (maybe checking for empty KEYTAB variable would be sufficient, since -e returns true for an empty variable).
 - Need better defaults for memory requests. When I tested, the catalog and Jenkins containers were killed due to OOM. 2048 for the catalog and 1024 for Jenkins worked for me.
 - I had to set JAVA_HOME in the catalog service json because the container and host didn’t have the same JAVA_HOME.
 - It would be good to include the service json needed to launch the catalog. We’d need to make the catalog Docker image version a variable for maven to fill in during the build process. Maybe the catalog could be one of the service examples, like sleeper and httpd.
 - Downloading from archive.apache.org is discouraged. Is there anything else we can do instead for the solr download?
 - Applications launched by the catalog are run as the root user (presumably because the catalog is a privileged container); at least that’s what is happening in insecure mode. The catalog should have user auth and launch the apps as the end user. I see there are already tickets open to address this issue.
 - We need to work out persistent storage for some of these services (including the catalog), or users will get a bad surprise when services or individual containers are restarted.
 - Restart doesn’t seem to work. Looks like it is issuing a start, so maybe should be named start instead. Restart implies stopping + starting.
 - Could we use AppAdminClient/ApiServiceClient instead of copying the getApiClient methods from ApiServiceClient to make the REST calls?
 - After deploying a service, it drops to the bottom of the list on the main catalog page. Is that intentional?
 - If you click on a UI link too early, it brings up a URL such as {{jenkins.${service_name}.${user}.${domain}:8080}}. It would be better to disallow clicking on the link until the variables are populated, if possible.

This seems to have broken the build on my system. I think we should revert both this and YARN-9348 until we have a fix.

[~ebadger] This doesn't appear to be a problem with the project, but the local docker configuration is disallowing contact to port 80 where docker is configured to build the image.  It looks like docker is running, but you don't have permission to run docker build command.  There are three ways to resolve this issue:

# Put yourself in docker group, and make sure you have rights to docker build command.
# run start-build-env.sh and do project build inside the docker environment.
# Shutdown docker to prevent docker image from building.

The first option will allow full build with docker images.  The second and third options are available if you don't want to do a full build and skip docker images building.

If all three options fails, please describe your environment to help with the troubleshoot of permission denied issue.

We shouldn't be building a docker image by default when we build hadoop. We should either write out a dockerfile that the user can use to build the docker image or we should add this as a mode that can be enabled. But this shouldn't be something enabled by default. 

I don't see why it is necessary to create an incompatible change for builds in this instance.

[~ebadger] {quote}We shouldn't be building a docker image by default when we build hadoop. We should either write out a dockerfile that the user can use to build the docker image or we should add this as a mode that can be enabled. But this shouldn't be something enabled by default.{quote}

This has been [discussed|http://mail-archives.apache.org/mod_mbox/hadoop-common-dev/201902.mbox/%3C9a375fba-6df3-8682-d48a-2367e522682d%40apache.org%3E] in Hadoop development list a while ago.  Out of 38 Apache projects that have official projects on Docker Hub, only 7 projects uses separate build process for docker images.  The majority of consensus is to have inline of the master build to ensure reproducibility.  Sorry, I tried best effort to keep this change non-disruptive, but it does introduce inconvenience for environment that has docker but user is disallowed to use docker.  Docker maven plugin does provide reasonable information on the root cause to ensure the requirement can be met.  I don't think this is an incompatible change but user must know how to read maven plugin error message.

bq. The majority of consensus is to have inline of the master build to ensure reproducibility

But why is this enabled by default? For anyone who doesn't want the docker image, it is just extra overhead in the build which takes time. That's on top of the fact that it broke my perfectly valid environment. I figured the reason behind the build breaking was a user permissions issue, but all of the options that you gave are unacceptable or unnecessary.

bq. 1. Put yourself in docker group, and make sure you have rights to docker build command.
I do not want to give my user access to the docker group. That should be reserved for a privileged user, since docker is a daemon running by root.

bq. 2. run start-build-env.sh and do project build inside the docker environment.
This step is unnecessary. I have never had to build hadoop inside of a docker environment and this should not be a requirement now. 

bq. 3. Shutdown docker to prevent docker image from building.
This is also unnecessary. Why should I have to start and stop my docker services every time I want to build? 

The tl;dr here is that my environment was able to build trunk before this patch and is not able to build trunk after this patch. My environment was not invalid and therefore should not be broken unless out of necessity, which I do not believe is the case here.

Making changes that break builds for some portion of the community seems unacceptable to me. This is poor Hadoop community etiquette. The correct solution, in my opinion, would be to only do the additional build steps if an additional flag is set. Don't break the way of building and then expect a lot of people to go fix it.

I agree with [~ebadger].  Suddenly today I am unable to build trunk.  Shouldn't a change this significant have been preceded by some kind of announcement?   The referenced discussion about Ozone did not alert me at all.  I honestly thought that discussion was about the automated apache builds.

Also, as [~ebadger] said, requiring me to either remove docker or change group settings for it is unreasonable.

I think there should be build switch or something to enable this mode of building.

[~eyang], 
bq. Put yourself in docker group, and make sure you have rights to docker build command.

From https://docs.docker.com/install/linux/linux-postinstall/

{noformat}
Warning
The docker group grants privileges equivalent to the root user. For details on how this impacts security in your system, see Docker Daemon Attack Surface.
{noformat}

Suggesting normal users to run builds with equivalent of root user access is a non-starter and severely limits users as well as provides a huge security risk. Suggesting docker group addition as a viable build setup is gross under-stating of the consequences.
Can this please be reverted first to unblock folks, before we address the issues and merge it back again?
Sure, reverting now. I am in favor of having a build flag for triggering the profile. This is an optional new YARN application and it doesn't need to require changes to the main build.

[~ebadger] [~epayne] [~vinodkv], I recognize the importance of not breaking developer's environment unless there is a good reason.  How about we introduce -DskipDocker in the same tune to skip shaded jars for avoiding certain features?

This is similar to shaded jar files that the build process happens by default in order to guarantee recursive dependent artifacts can all be signed.  Inline build process is the only proper solution to ensure the build is reproducible and properly signed.  If we have intend to ensure that we can release docker image with signed hadoop jar file embedded in the docker image, and then sign the docker image for release.  I think docker build should be on by default with option to turn off.

Using profile to generate different artifact is strongly discouraged practice by [Maven best practice guide line|https://dzone.com/articles/maven-profile-best-practices].  There are important merits in the guideline to avoid build/release problems for maven repository.  This is the reason that I did not use profile to activate docker build profile.  Let me know your thoughts and there might be better way to resolve this issue than revert.  Thanks

[~jeagles] {quote}Suggesting normal users to run builds with equivalent of root user access is a non-starter and severely limits users as well as provides a huge security risk. Suggesting docker group addition as a viable build setup is gross under-stating of the consequences.{quote}

Multiple options are available to deactivate Docker image build.  I still think it is important to have ability to generate docker images without a separate build process.  There are voices in dev to vote against inline docker build process for Hadoop, and I am open to smooth out imperfection on growing pains.  Let me know if you have input on make this process better.  I would hate to see that we don't put in effort, and abandon inline docker build process and let community to grow like weed.

I don't think -DskipDocker is the correct approach here. This gets us back to the exact same problem. Everyone who was building trunk just fine will now have to change their build approach. 

bq. This is similar to shaded jar files that the build process happens by default in order to guarantee recursive dependent artifacts can all be signed.
I could make the same argument about native code. Not everyone needs the native code in their builds. If we added that in by default then the build time would slow down a lot for no real reason. Release managers know that they should build the native code and so they do. In the same way, they can enable this docker profile to the build when they are doing a release. 

bq. Using profile to generate different artifact is strongly discouraged practice by Maven best practice guide line.
I'm not quite sure what the validity of this best practices guide is, but one of the first things stated is "Good practice is to minimise the effort required to make a successful build." Forcing all developers to add a -D flag to their builds to maintain what they had before this patch is the opposite of minimizing effort. Only release managers or those dealing directly with this feature will need the docker image. Everybody that isn't using the feature shouldn't have to change because of that small minority.

{quote}Now, Hadoop build process intended to introduce inline docker build using signed jar files.
{quote}
[~eyang] based on the discussion you referenced above, two Jiras were filed: HADOOP-16092 and HADOOP-16091
 Isn't that where this discussion about changing the build process should be?  Those have not been resolved, so I don't understand why we are making this change here?

[~Jim_Brennan] Those issues are Ozone specific, and filed on behave of Ozone.  This issue has been around much longer and the problem can be solved using proper maven design flow.  Jenkins and Maven are designed to run maven release process without calling profiles, this is the reason that I didn't just add -Pdocker to make this issue go away for dev.  There are already a few offenders native/yarn-ui/dist.  The incorrect procedures don't scale in the long run for release process.  It might be useful to learn the correct solutions than bash release manager's head against Jenkins.

bq. Forcing all developers to add a -D flag to their builds to maintain what they had before this patch is the opposite of minimizing effort. Only release managers or those dealing directly with this feature will need the docker image. Everybody that isn't using the feature shouldn't have to change because of that small minority.
[~ebadger], I agree wholeheartedly. This is my feeling also.

bq. Multiple options are available to deactivate Docker image build
If a build feature is disabled a majority of the time, the default should be off.
bq. Using profile to generate different artifact is strongly discouraged practice by Maven best practice guide line
I generally agree with this, which is I would argue creating a docker image should be a separate process, possibly even project.

From a technical perspective, does the docker image evolve at the same rate as the project? Is there a 1-to-1 relationship between build artifacts (i.e. would multiple docker images be viable for a single  hadoop release. conversely, would a single docker image be viable for multiple hadoop release). 

From a community perspective, does feature restrict who can be a release manager? Does this work out of the box for per build instructions? Do we compromise the security of developers by enabling this feature?

From a process perspective, this jira isn't following best practices. An umbrella jira shouldn't be closed before its subtasks. With a 1MB+ patch, I wonder if we are combining multiple features into a single jira.

[~eyang], I do have a few question for you so that we can avoid revert work in the future. Did you feel there was community consensus or contention before this went in? Did the right people look at the jira before commit. Personally, the title and description were not enough for me to know what a large change it was. But specifically, I wonder what you thought could help.

[~jeagles] Majority of this patch are binary image files.  [~billie.rinaldi] has been a great Hadoop committer, and this was committed with her thorough reviews.  The actual code is small compare to other patches.  What makes this subproject unique is this project is the first example application to run in a docker container in YARN framework.  Hadoop have several examples of running docker containers, but none of the examples are actually built by Hadoop project.  It was intended to be a good model for others to clone for building more YARN applications.  Obviously, I failed in making assumption that developers often use start-build-dev.sh script to start the build environment.  My own encounter with separate build process for docker image has not been positive so far in the past 4 years.  There is too many build caches, source code tagging and race conditions that would impair good release quality in my experience.  This is the reason that I push for inline build process, even if it is much harder to accomplish.  The intend is to save developer time to avoid the release mine fields and as good development experience as possible.

FYI, the patch 28 committed code already support -DskipDocker, and developer can configure explicitly to skip docker build in user's own maven settings.xml:

{code}
<profile>
    <id>skip.docker.by.default</id>
    <activation>
        <activeByDefault>true</activeByDefault>
    </activation>
    <properties>
        <skipDocker>true</skipDocker>
    </properties>
</profile>
{code}

Hadoop [BUILD.txt|https://github.com/apache/hadoop/blame/trunk/BUILDING.txt] has mentioned to use docker for build.  This instruction was committed 4 years ago.  Therefore, docker assumption is not new for developer environment.  [~ebadger] does this sound like a reasonable workaround to reduce impact to your development environment?

bq. Eric Badger does this sound like a reasonable workaround to reduce impact to your development environment?
No. This is exactly what you had in the patch that was committed that broke my build environment. The whole point here is that I shouldn't need a workaround to maintain my working build environment.

[~eyang] and all, let's keep discussions civil and focused on the technical aspects. Everyone's time and contributions here are valuable, including the dissenting opinions. And all help to build a better product for the community.

I can see we are failing to meet the requirements of individuals on this feature. Let's bring this discussion to the attention of the mailing list, which brings the focus on the proper stake holders. There are aspects of this jira that touch on the philosophy of development and release management, security concerns, maintenance concerns, proper JIRA process, as well as increasing the scope of YARN deliverables to including publishing and maintaining docker images.

We need this need to bring these technical discussions to the community and look at these objectively.

{quote} From a process perspective, this jira isn't following best practices. An umbrella jira shouldn't be closed before its subtasks. With a 1MB+ patch, I wonder if we are combining multiple features into a single jira.{quote}

[~eyang], I may have missed it, but I don't feel this comment was addressed. Are we still bundling multiple features into this jira? What I see is that there is still a 1MB+ patch and looks like we are trying to commit to a top level umbrella jira.

[~jeagles] {quote}I may have missed it, but I don't feel this comment was addressed. Are we still bundling multiple features into this jira? What I see is that there is still a 1MB+ patch and looks like we are trying to commit to a top level umbrella jira.{quote}

Most of the patch is in fonts directory and a class that generates random English sound alike words.  The actual code is small when those pieces are removed. 
 The rest of the code basically setup maven project framework for each of the necessary pieces to go into sub modules.  Patch size is not a good metric to review New Feature patches.  There are many examples of New Feature issues with big patch, e.g. YARN-913, YARN-2928, HADOOP-8562 and many others.  [~billie.rinaldi] has done the due-diligence in reviewing the patch with detail step by step improvements and had committed when basic structure is working.

The people who are interested in the application catalog have already done the reviews and acquired the knowledge of inner work of this patch.  Breaking down the patch further at this point in time, would not help the community other than introducing additional logistic overhead to check for correctness that had already been checked.  People have already been involved and able to reproduce.  Clear javadoc and documentation are also included for a good starting trajectory.  

If you think otherwise, please list details of how you want the refactoring done.  I will comply and request your reviews.

Failed unit test is not related to patch 032.  [~ebadger] can you review if this unblock your development flow?

* versions of artifacts in the webapp pom should be taken from the hadoop project uber-pom; so maintained in sync. mockito, is that central pom already, for example
* same for all the maven plugin versions. If they are new plugins, add the property to the hadoop-project jar and then reference it.
* Not reviewing the code, trusting you all there.

Is there a way to have some example which doesn't add large amounts of binary data? Because its going to make our repo even bigger, increase the time it takes to switch across branches slower, etc -stuff I do do regularly. Git isn't a place to keep binaries

[~eyang], excuse me since I'm not exactly sure, but does this patch add a new dependencies on apache solr and therefore lucene, transitively. If so, does this impact customers already having a solr or lucene dependencies?

In addition, I see this patch adds another class named YarnClient. Is there a way to name this class differently to remove this conflation?

My goal isn't to review this code, since that has already been done. But the whole community needs to support it, myself included, and to understand what parts might impact its users.

Patch 033 includes [~stevel@apache.org] and [~jeagles] feedbacks.

Time benchmark for building application catalog on a 2015 i7 MacBook Pro with Redhat 7 as guest vm.
Benchmark command in hadoop-yarn-applications-catalog folder:

{code}
mvn clean package -Pdocker
{code}

Fresh checkout with no docker cache:
{code}
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
[INFO] Reactor Summary:
[INFO] 
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog ........................... SUCCESS [  1.090 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Webapp .................... SUCCESS [ 23.444 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Docker Image .............. SUCCESS [02:19 min]
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

[INFO] Finished at: 2019-03-22T15:16:19-04:00
[INFO] Final Memory: 108M/906M
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
{code}

After maven and docker cache are built once:
{code}
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
[INFO] Reactor Summary:

[INFO] YARN Application Catalog ........................... SUCCESS [  1.268 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Webapp .................... SUCCESS [ 24.019 s]

[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

Skipping docker build (mvn clean package):
{code}
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

[INFO] YARN Application Catalog ........................... SUCCESS [  1.151 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Webapp .................... SUCCESS [ 25.091 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Docker Image .............. SUCCESS [  0.144 s]
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
[INFO] BUILD SUCCESS
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

[INFO] Finished at: 2019-03-22T14:35:18-04:00
[INFO] Final Memory: 87M/917M
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
{code}

Base on the result, it's good evidence to say that if developer can be patient on fresh build once.  Having docker build inline does not introduce much overhead for developer that needs to rapidly reiterate building process.

When using profile to generate artifact, some part of code might not be exercised daily, and hide bugs from surfacing until release time.  I hope Hadoop community will consider to make docker build process inline some day.

I'm +0 on the patch in its current state. I tested out the patch and it does not disrupt my build environment. 

However, I still don't like the idea of committing a patch on an umbrella JIRA. We can't close the JIRA because it has open subtasks. So I think that we should create a subtask and add this patch to it.

[~ebadger] Thanks for the reply.  Hadoop Bylaws does not state that patch can not commit to umbrella JIRA.  There are other new features issues that have developed in similar fashion, committed in top level patch, then refined in sub-tasks.  The preference is noted, but given the discussion here to use profile to build docker image.  I think it is important to preserve the discussion history to prevent future developers from resenting docker image profile builds.

[~jeagles] Thanks for the hint on fixedVersion.  I see what you are saying and will remove fixed version, and put the issue into in progress mode.  This is aligned with YARN-913, HDFS-7240 where patches are committed but subtasks are on-going.

In the future, when people are looking for reason why Hadoop use profile to make docker build optional, this thread provides good reference to historical document why we land on this decision.  If the code is added to another subtask, then the reasoning and lesson learn will be lost.  Future developers will not understand why we choose this route.  There is no intend to make either party feel bad about the out come, but hope that experience gained in this issue can help other people/projects to make well informed decision.

If you are looking for a tactical solution to 1) keep relevant discussion with this jira and 2) be able to close this jira to address [~ebadger]'s comment why not create a new umbrella ticket and make all sub tasks and well as this jira a subtask of the new umbrella. Perhaps re-summarizing this JIRA to reflect it having core functionality.

[~ebadger] I just step away for a couple hours to live life.  I don't expect to return to this issue to find comment like this.  I am open to convert this issue to a subtask.  Please don't make it hard for others to work with you.  Thanks
Looks like discussion has resolved here. I am planning to commit patch 35 + YARN-9348. Thanks for the patch, [~eyang], as well as everyone who contributed to the discussion!

status.name: Resolved

comments.body: Appstore concept can be a standalone Apache project on its own.  Before proposing this as independent project, I like to gather input from community to see if this feature is worthwhile to implement as a YARN sub-project or it would be better to developed as an independent project.

Thanks for working on this, [~eyang]. The catalog looks like a promising feature. I have started reviewing patch 18. Here is a first round of comments:
 - Service catalog might be a better name, since the catalog handles YARN services and not arbitrary applications. In that case I’d suggest moving the module to hadoop-yarn-applications/hadoop-yarn-services/hadoop-yarn-services-catalog.
 - NOTICE says “binary distribution of this product bundles binaries of ___” but it should be “source distribution bundles ___.” Also, this information should go in LICENSE instead of NOTICE for non-ASLv2 permissive licenses (see [http://www.apache.org/dev/licensing-howto.html#permissive-deps]). It would be helpful to include the path(s) to the dependencies, so it’s easier to tell when included source code has proper handling in LICENSE and NOTICE and when it doesn’t. I have not checked whether all the external code added in this patch has been included properly in L&N yet.
 - There are files named bootstrap-hwx.js and bootstrap-hwx.min.js.
 - 8080 is a difficult port. If you ran the catalog on an Ambari-installed cluster, it would fail if it came up on the Ambari server host. Does the app catalog need to have net=host? If not, the port wouldn’t be a problem.
 - I don’t think we should set net=host for the examples, because that setting is not recommended for most containers. Also, for the services using 8080, they could run into conflicts when net=host.
 - I think it would be better to have fewer examples in samples.xml and make sure that they all work (the pcjs image doesn’t exist on dockerhub, and we might not want to seem to be “endorsing” all the example images that are currently included). Maybe just include httpd and Jenkins? Registry would also be a reasonable candidate, but it didn’t work when I tried it with insecure limit users mode (it failed to make a dir when pushing an image to it; maybe would work as a privileged container with image name library/registry:latest).
 - entrypoint.sh needs modifications to make insecure mode possible (maybe checking for empty KEYTAB variable would be sufficient, since -e returns true for an empty variable).
 - Need better defaults for memory requests. When I tested, the catalog and Jenkins containers were killed due to OOM. 2048 for the catalog and 1024 for Jenkins worked for me.
 - I had to set JAVA_HOME in the catalog service json because the container and host didn’t have the same JAVA_HOME.
 - It would be good to include the service json needed to launch the catalog. We’d need to make the catalog Docker image version a variable for maven to fill in during the build process. Maybe the catalog could be one of the service examples, like sleeper and httpd.
 - Downloading from archive.apache.org is discouraged. Is there anything else we can do instead for the solr download?
 - Applications launched by the catalog are run as the root user (presumably because the catalog is a privileged container); at least that’s what is happening in insecure mode. The catalog should have user auth and launch the apps as the end user. I see there are already tickets open to address this issue.
 - We need to work out persistent storage for some of these services (including the catalog), or users will get a bad surprise when services or individual containers are restarted.
 - Restart doesn’t seem to work. Looks like it is issuing a start, so maybe should be named start instead. Restart implies stopping + starting.
 - Could we use AppAdminClient/ApiServiceClient instead of copying the getApiClient methods from ApiServiceClient to make the REST calls?
 - After deploying a service, it drops to the bottom of the list on the main catalog page. Is that intentional?
 - If you click on a UI link too early, it brings up a URL such as {{jenkins.${service_name}.${user}.${domain}:8080}}. It would be better to disallow clicking on the link until the variables are populated, if possible.

This seems to have broken the build on my system. I think we should revert both this and YARN-9348 until we have a fix.

[~ebadger] This doesn't appear to be a problem with the project, but the local docker configuration is disallowing contact to port 80 where docker is configured to build the image.  It looks like docker is running, but you don't have permission to run docker build command.  There are three ways to resolve this issue:

# Put yourself in docker group, and make sure you have rights to docker build command.
# run start-build-env.sh and do project build inside the docker environment.
# Shutdown docker to prevent docker image from building.

The first option will allow full build with docker images.  The second and third options are available if you don't want to do a full build and skip docker images building.

If all three options fails, please describe your environment to help with the troubleshoot of permission denied issue.

We shouldn't be building a docker image by default when we build hadoop. We should either write out a dockerfile that the user can use to build the docker image or we should add this as a mode that can be enabled. But this shouldn't be something enabled by default. 

I don't see why it is necessary to create an incompatible change for builds in this instance.

[~ebadger] {quote}We shouldn't be building a docker image by default when we build hadoop. We should either write out a dockerfile that the user can use to build the docker image or we should add this as a mode that can be enabled. But this shouldn't be something enabled by default.{quote}

This has been [discussed|http://mail-archives.apache.org/mod_mbox/hadoop-common-dev/201902.mbox/%3C9a375fba-6df3-8682-d48a-2367e522682d%40apache.org%3E] in Hadoop development list a while ago.  Out of 38 Apache projects that have official projects on Docker Hub, only 7 projects uses separate build process for docker images.  The majority of consensus is to have inline of the master build to ensure reproducibility.  Sorry, I tried best effort to keep this change non-disruptive, but it does introduce inconvenience for environment that has docker but user is disallowed to use docker.  Docker maven plugin does provide reasonable information on the root cause to ensure the requirement can be met.  I don't think this is an incompatible change but user must know how to read maven plugin error message.

bq. The majority of consensus is to have inline of the master build to ensure reproducibility

But why is this enabled by default? For anyone who doesn't want the docker image, it is just extra overhead in the build which takes time. That's on top of the fact that it broke my perfectly valid environment. I figured the reason behind the build breaking was a user permissions issue, but all of the options that you gave are unacceptable or unnecessary.

bq. 1. Put yourself in docker group, and make sure you have rights to docker build command.
I do not want to give my user access to the docker group. That should be reserved for a privileged user, since docker is a daemon running by root.

bq. 2. run start-build-env.sh and do project build inside the docker environment.
This step is unnecessary. I have never had to build hadoop inside of a docker environment and this should not be a requirement now. 

bq. 3. Shutdown docker to prevent docker image from building.
This is also unnecessary. Why should I have to start and stop my docker services every time I want to build? 

The tl;dr here is that my environment was able to build trunk before this patch and is not able to build trunk after this patch. My environment was not invalid and therefore should not be broken unless out of necessity, which I do not believe is the case here.

Making changes that break builds for some portion of the community seems unacceptable to me. This is poor Hadoop community etiquette. The correct solution, in my opinion, would be to only do the additional build steps if an additional flag is set. Don't break the way of building and then expect a lot of people to go fix it.

I agree with [~ebadger].  Suddenly today I am unable to build trunk.  Shouldn't a change this significant have been preceded by some kind of announcement?   The referenced discussion about Ozone did not alert me at all.  I honestly thought that discussion was about the automated apache builds.

Also, as [~ebadger] said, requiring me to either remove docker or change group settings for it is unreasonable.

I think there should be build switch or something to enable this mode of building.

[~eyang], 
bq. Put yourself in docker group, and make sure you have rights to docker build command.

From https://docs.docker.com/install/linux/linux-postinstall/

{noformat}
Warning
The docker group grants privileges equivalent to the root user. For details on how this impacts security in your system, see Docker Daemon Attack Surface.
{noformat}

Suggesting normal users to run builds with equivalent of root user access is a non-starter and severely limits users as well as provides a huge security risk. Suggesting docker group addition as a viable build setup is gross under-stating of the consequences.
Can this please be reverted first to unblock folks, before we address the issues and merge it back again?
Sure, reverting now. I am in favor of having a build flag for triggering the profile. This is an optional new YARN application and it doesn't need to require changes to the main build.

[~ebadger] [~epayne] [~vinodkv], I recognize the importance of not breaking developer's environment unless there is a good reason.  How about we introduce -DskipDocker in the same tune to skip shaded jars for avoiding certain features?

This is similar to shaded jar files that the build process happens by default in order to guarantee recursive dependent artifacts can all be signed.  Inline build process is the only proper solution to ensure the build is reproducible and properly signed.  If we have intend to ensure that we can release docker image with signed hadoop jar file embedded in the docker image, and then sign the docker image for release.  I think docker build should be on by default with option to turn off.

Using profile to generate different artifact is strongly discouraged practice by [Maven best practice guide line|https://dzone.com/articles/maven-profile-best-practices].  There are important merits in the guideline to avoid build/release problems for maven repository.  This is the reason that I did not use profile to activate docker build profile.  Let me know your thoughts and there might be better way to resolve this issue than revert.  Thanks

[~jeagles] {quote}Suggesting normal users to run builds with equivalent of root user access is a non-starter and severely limits users as well as provides a huge security risk. Suggesting docker group addition as a viable build setup is gross under-stating of the consequences.{quote}

Multiple options are available to deactivate Docker image build.  I still think it is important to have ability to generate docker images without a separate build process.  There are voices in dev to vote against inline docker build process for Hadoop, and I am open to smooth out imperfection on growing pains.  Let me know if you have input on make this process better.  I would hate to see that we don't put in effort, and abandon inline docker build process and let community to grow like weed.

I don't think -DskipDocker is the correct approach here. This gets us back to the exact same problem. Everyone who was building trunk just fine will now have to change their build approach. 

bq. This is similar to shaded jar files that the build process happens by default in order to guarantee recursive dependent artifacts can all be signed.
I could make the same argument about native code. Not everyone needs the native code in their builds. If we added that in by default then the build time would slow down a lot for no real reason. Release managers know that they should build the native code and so they do. In the same way, they can enable this docker profile to the build when they are doing a release. 

bq. Using profile to generate different artifact is strongly discouraged practice by Maven best practice guide line.
I'm not quite sure what the validity of this best practices guide is, but one of the first things stated is "Good practice is to minimise the effort required to make a successful build." Forcing all developers to add a -D flag to their builds to maintain what they had before this patch is the opposite of minimizing effort. Only release managers or those dealing directly with this feature will need the docker image. Everybody that isn't using the feature shouldn't have to change because of that small minority.

{quote}Now, Hadoop build process intended to introduce inline docker build using signed jar files.
{quote}
[~eyang] based on the discussion you referenced above, two Jiras were filed: HADOOP-16092 and HADOOP-16091
 Isn't that where this discussion about changing the build process should be?  Those have not been resolved, so I don't understand why we are making this change here?

[~Jim_Brennan] Those issues are Ozone specific, and filed on behave of Ozone.  This issue has been around much longer and the problem can be solved using proper maven design flow.  Jenkins and Maven are designed to run maven release process without calling profiles, this is the reason that I didn't just add -Pdocker to make this issue go away for dev.  There are already a few offenders native/yarn-ui/dist.  The incorrect procedures don't scale in the long run for release process.  It might be useful to learn the correct solutions than bash release manager's head against Jenkins.

bq. Forcing all developers to add a -D flag to their builds to maintain what they had before this patch is the opposite of minimizing effort. Only release managers or those dealing directly with this feature will need the docker image. Everybody that isn't using the feature shouldn't have to change because of that small minority.
[~ebadger], I agree wholeheartedly. This is my feeling also.

bq. Multiple options are available to deactivate Docker image build
If a build feature is disabled a majority of the time, the default should be off.
bq. Using profile to generate different artifact is strongly discouraged practice by Maven best practice guide line
I generally agree with this, which is I would argue creating a docker image should be a separate process, possibly even project.

From a technical perspective, does the docker image evolve at the same rate as the project? Is there a 1-to-1 relationship between build artifacts (i.e. would multiple docker images be viable for a single  hadoop release. conversely, would a single docker image be viable for multiple hadoop release). 

From a community perspective, does feature restrict who can be a release manager? Does this work out of the box for per build instructions? Do we compromise the security of developers by enabling this feature?

From a process perspective, this jira isn't following best practices. An umbrella jira shouldn't be closed before its subtasks. With a 1MB+ patch, I wonder if we are combining multiple features into a single jira.

[~eyang], I do have a few question for you so that we can avoid revert work in the future. Did you feel there was community consensus or contention before this went in? Did the right people look at the jira before commit. Personally, the title and description were not enough for me to know what a large change it was. But specifically, I wonder what you thought could help.

[~jeagles] Majority of this patch are binary image files.  [~billie.rinaldi] has been a great Hadoop committer, and this was committed with her thorough reviews.  The actual code is small compare to other patches.  What makes this subproject unique is this project is the first example application to run in a docker container in YARN framework.  Hadoop have several examples of running docker containers, but none of the examples are actually built by Hadoop project.  It was intended to be a good model for others to clone for building more YARN applications.  Obviously, I failed in making assumption that developers often use start-build-dev.sh script to start the build environment.  My own encounter with separate build process for docker image has not been positive so far in the past 4 years.  There is too many build caches, source code tagging and race conditions that would impair good release quality in my experience.  This is the reason that I push for inline build process, even if it is much harder to accomplish.  The intend is to save developer time to avoid the release mine fields and as good development experience as possible.

FYI, the patch 28 committed code already support -DskipDocker, and developer can configure explicitly to skip docker build in user's own maven settings.xml:

{code}
<profile>
    <id>skip.docker.by.default</id>
    <activation>
        <activeByDefault>true</activeByDefault>
    </activation>
    <properties>
        <skipDocker>true</skipDocker>
    </properties>
</profile>
{code}

Hadoop [BUILD.txt|https://github.com/apache/hadoop/blame/trunk/BUILDING.txt] has mentioned to use docker for build.  This instruction was committed 4 years ago.  Therefore, docker assumption is not new for developer environment.  [~ebadger] does this sound like a reasonable workaround to reduce impact to your development environment?

bq. Eric Badger does this sound like a reasonable workaround to reduce impact to your development environment?
No. This is exactly what you had in the patch that was committed that broke my build environment. The whole point here is that I shouldn't need a workaround to maintain my working build environment.

[~eyang] and all, let's keep discussions civil and focused on the technical aspects. Everyone's time and contributions here are valuable, including the dissenting opinions. And all help to build a better product for the community.

I can see we are failing to meet the requirements of individuals on this feature. Let's bring this discussion to the attention of the mailing list, which brings the focus on the proper stake holders. There are aspects of this jira that touch on the philosophy of development and release management, security concerns, maintenance concerns, proper JIRA process, as well as increasing the scope of YARN deliverables to including publishing and maintaining docker images.

We need this need to bring these technical discussions to the community and look at these objectively.

{quote} From a process perspective, this jira isn't following best practices. An umbrella jira shouldn't be closed before its subtasks. With a 1MB+ patch, I wonder if we are combining multiple features into a single jira.{quote}

[~eyang], I may have missed it, but I don't feel this comment was addressed. Are we still bundling multiple features into this jira? What I see is that there is still a 1MB+ patch and looks like we are trying to commit to a top level umbrella jira.

[~jeagles] {quote}I may have missed it, but I don't feel this comment was addressed. Are we still bundling multiple features into this jira? What I see is that there is still a 1MB+ patch and looks like we are trying to commit to a top level umbrella jira.{quote}

Most of the patch is in fonts directory and a class that generates random English sound alike words.  The actual code is small when those pieces are removed. 
 The rest of the code basically setup maven project framework for each of the necessary pieces to go into sub modules.  Patch size is not a good metric to review New Feature patches.  There are many examples of New Feature issues with big patch, e.g. YARN-913, YARN-2928, HADOOP-8562 and many others.  [~billie.rinaldi] has done the due-diligence in reviewing the patch with detail step by step improvements and had committed when basic structure is working.

The people who are interested in the application catalog have already done the reviews and acquired the knowledge of inner work of this patch.  Breaking down the patch further at this point in time, would not help the community other than introducing additional logistic overhead to check for correctness that had already been checked.  People have already been involved and able to reproduce.  Clear javadoc and documentation are also included for a good starting trajectory.  

If you think otherwise, please list details of how you want the refactoring done.  I will comply and request your reviews.

Failed unit test is not related to patch 032.  [~ebadger] can you review if this unblock your development flow?

* versions of artifacts in the webapp pom should be taken from the hadoop project uber-pom; so maintained in sync. mockito, is that central pom already, for example
* same for all the maven plugin versions. If they are new plugins, add the property to the hadoop-project jar and then reference it.
* Not reviewing the code, trusting you all there.

Is there a way to have some example which doesn't add large amounts of binary data? Because its going to make our repo even bigger, increase the time it takes to switch across branches slower, etc -stuff I do do regularly. Git isn't a place to keep binaries

[~eyang], excuse me since I'm not exactly sure, but does this patch add a new dependencies on apache solr and therefore lucene, transitively. If so, does this impact customers already having a solr or lucene dependencies?

In addition, I see this patch adds another class named YarnClient. Is there a way to name this class differently to remove this conflation?

My goal isn't to review this code, since that has already been done. But the whole community needs to support it, myself included, and to understand what parts might impact its users.

Patch 033 includes [~stevel@apache.org] and [~jeagles] feedbacks.

Time benchmark for building application catalog on a 2015 i7 MacBook Pro with Redhat 7 as guest vm.
Benchmark command in hadoop-yarn-applications-catalog folder:

{code}
mvn clean package -Pdocker
{code}

Fresh checkout with no docker cache:
{code}
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
[INFO] Reactor Summary:
[INFO] 
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog ........................... SUCCESS [  1.090 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Webapp .................... SUCCESS [ 23.444 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Docker Image .............. SUCCESS [02:19 min]
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

[INFO] Finished at: 2019-03-22T15:16:19-04:00
[INFO] Final Memory: 108M/906M
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
{code}

After maven and docker cache are built once:
{code}
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
[INFO] Reactor Summary:

[INFO] YARN Application Catalog ........................... SUCCESS [  1.268 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Webapp .................... SUCCESS [ 24.019 s]

[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

Skipping docker build (mvn clean package):
{code}
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

[INFO] YARN Application Catalog ........................... SUCCESS [  1.151 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Webapp .................... SUCCESS [ 25.091 s]
[INFO] YARN Application Catalog Docker Image .............. SUCCESS [  0.144 s]
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
[INFO] BUILD SUCCESS
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------

[INFO] Finished at: 2019-03-22T14:35:18-04:00
[INFO] Final Memory: 87M/917M
[INFO] ------------------------------------------------------------------------
{code}

Base on the result, it's good evidence to say that if developer can be patient on fresh build once.  Having docker build inline does not introduce much overhead for developer that needs to rapidly reiterate building process.

When using profile to generate artifact, some part of code might not be exercised daily, and hide bugs from surfacing until release time.  I hope Hadoop community will consider to make docker build process inline some day.

I'm +0 on the patch in its current state. I tested out the patch and it does not disrupt my build environment. 

However, I still don't like the idea of committing a patch on an umbrella JIRA. We can't close the JIRA because it has open subtasks. So I think that we should create a subtask and add this patch to it.

[~ebadger] Thanks for the reply.  Hadoop Bylaws does not state that patch can not commit to umbrella JIRA.  There are other new features issues that have developed in similar fashion, committed in top level patch, then refined in sub-tasks.  The preference is noted, but given the discussion here to use profile to build docker image.  I think it is important to preserve the discussion history to prevent future developers from resenting docker image profile builds.

[~jeagles] Thanks for the hint on fixedVersion.  I see what you are saying and will remove fixed version, and put the issue into in progress mode.  This is aligned with YARN-913, HDFS-7240 where patches are committed but subtasks are on-going.

In the future, when people are looking for reason why Hadoop use profile to make docker build optional, this thread provides good reference to historical document why we land on this decision.  If the code is added to another subtask, then the reasoning and lesson learn will be lost.  Future developers will not understand why we choose this route.  There is no intend to make either party feel bad about the out come, but hope that experience gained in this issue can help other people/projects to make well informed decision.

If you are looking for a tactical solution to 1) keep relevant discussion with this jira and 2) be able to close this jira to address [~ebadger]'s comment why not create a new umbrella ticket and make all sub tasks and well as this jira a subtask of the new umbrella. Perhaps re-summarizing this JIRA to reflect it having core functionality.

[~ebadger] I just step away for a couple hours to live life.  I don't expect to return to this issue to find comment like this.  I am open to convert this issue to a subtask.  Please don't make it hard for others to work with you.  Thanks
Looks like discussion has resolved here. I am planning to commit patch 35 + YARN-9348. Thanks for the patch, [~eyang], as well as everyone who contributed to the discussion!
